subject: Physics
solution:
Total volume of rain drops, received $100 \mathrm{~cm}$ in a year by area $1 \mathrm{~m}^2$
$$
=1 \mathrm{~m}^2 \times \frac{100}{100} \mathrm{~m}=1 \mathrm{~m}^3
$$
As we know, density of water,
$$
d=10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3
$$
Therefore, mass of this volume of water $M=d \times v=10^3 \times 1=10^3 \mathrm{~kg}$
Average terminal velocity of rain drop $v=9 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ (given)
Therefore, energy transferred by rain,
$$
\begin{aligned}
&E=\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \\
&=\frac{1}{2} \times 10^3 \times(9)^2 \\
&=\frac{1}{2} \times 10^3 \times 81=4.05 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
solution:
As the bubble is rising steadily the net force acting on it will be zero.

(Because of density of air is negligible, so the value of mg can be neglected)

So, the force of buoyancy B is equal to the weight of the displaced liquid acting upward is equal to the viscous force acting downward, i.e., B=F.

⇒4π3R3ρg=6πηRv

Putting R=1 mm=10-3 m, ρ=1.75×103 kg m-3, g=10 m s-2 and v=0.35×10-2 m s-1

η=109=1·11

In SI unit, η=11 poise CGS.

Number.

From NCERT 

If Re<1000 → flow is steady

1000<Rp<2000→ flow becomes unsteady

Re>2000→ flow is turbulent

subject: Physics
solution:
Let the radius of the drop at time t=t be r and at an instant t=t+dt be r-dr

As surface area is given by, A=4πr2,

∴ decrease in surface area is,

dA=4π2rdr

∴ Decrease in surface energy during time dt is,

dU=TdA=T·8πrdr     ...1

Decrease in volume during time dt is,

dV=4πr2dr

∴ Decrease in mass during time dt is,

dm=ρdV=4πρr2dr

∴ Heat required in vaporisation is,

dQ=Ldm=4πρr2drL    ...2

Apply conservation of energy,

decrease in surface energy=heat required in vaporisation.

⇒ dU=dQ

⇒ 8Tπrdr=4πρr2drL

⇒2T=ρrL

⇒r=2TρL

Hence, the minimum radius is 2TρL

​​​​​​​

Then, when body moves with constant velocity,

FV+FB=Mg   

Or, FV=Mg-FB

FV=ρBVg-ρLVg =ρB-ρLVg, where, V is the volume of small ball.

=8-1.3×103×0.3×10-38×103×10

=6.7×0.38×10-2

=67×38×10-4=25.125×10-4=25×10-4 N

Thus, the value of x=25.

solution: Weight of the body $\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{mg}=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3 \rho \mathrm{g}$ $\mathrm{T}=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3 \sigma \mathrm{g}$ and $\mathrm{F}=6 \pi \eta \mathrm{vr}$ When the body attains terminal velocity net force acting on the body is zero. i.e., $\mathrm{W}-\mathrm{T}-\mathrm{F}=0$ And terminal velocity $\mathrm{v}=\frac{2}{9} \frac{\mathrm{r}^2(\rho-\sigma) \mathrm{g}}{\eta}$ As in case of upward motion upward force is twice its effective weight, therefore, it will move with same speed $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$

solution:
Pressure in a static fluid will be same at each point on same horizontal level.

P=P0+ρgh

where P0 is atmospheric pressure.

Pascal's law states that pressure applied to enclosed water is transmitted equally in all directions. Hence, both the statements are true and this is the right option.

subject: Physics
solution:
The buoyant force is given by 

B=Vρ0g

where V is the volume of the ball. The weight of the ball

W=Mg=Vρg, is acting in the opposite direction of the buoyant force . Hence, at terminal velocity

Mg-B=Fv

⇒B+Fv=Mg

⇒Fv=Vρg-Vρ0g⇒Fv=ρVg(1-ρ0ρ)⇒Fv=Mg(1-ρ0ρ)

subject: Physics
solution:
The terminal velocity $v_0$ of a spherical body falling through a viscous liquid is given by Stokes' Law:
$v_0 = \frac{2r^2(\sigma - \rho)g}{9\eta}$
Rearranging the formula to express viscosity $\eta$ in terms of other variables:
$\eta = \frac{2r^2(\sigma - \rho)g}{9v_0}$
Since we are told to ignore errors associated with $\sigma$, $\rho$, and $g$, these quantities along with the numerical factor $\frac{2}{9}$ are treated as constants.
The expression for $\eta$ can be written as $\eta = k \cdot \frac{r^2}{v_0}$, where $k = \frac{2(\sigma - \rho)g}{9}$ is a constant.
Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:
$\ln \eta = \ln k + 2\ln r - \ln v_0$
Differentiating both sides to find the relative error:
$\frac{d\eta}{\eta} = 0 + 2\frac{dr}{r} - \frac{dv_0}{v_0}$
For the estimated maximum fractional error, we take the absolute sum of the individual relative errors:
$\frac{\Delta \eta}{\eta} = 2\frac{\Delta r}{r} + \frac{\Delta v_0}{v_0}$
This matches option (1).
